attributes.stem: A 65-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with progressive shortness of breath on exertion over the past two years. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with a pulse rate of 88/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. His chest is hyperexpanded with decreased breath sounds throughout and prolonged expiration. Spirometry is performed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following spirometry findings is most consistent with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of COPD including progressive dyspnoea, significant smoking history, and examination findings of hyperexpansion and prolonged expiration. The hallmark spirometry finding in COPD is a decreased FEV1/FVC ratio, indicating airflow obstruction that is not fully reversible. This occurs because the forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1) is reduced proportionally more than the forced vital capacity (FVC).

A normal FEV1/FVC ratio would suggest normal lung function or a restrictive pattern, neither of which are consistent with COPD.

An increased FEV1/FVC ratio is physiologically unlikely and would not occur in any common respiratory condition.

Normal total lung capacity would not be expected in COPD. Instead, total lung capacity is typically increased due to air trapping and hyperinflation.

Decreased residual volume is not seen in COPD. Due to air trapping, residual volume is characteristically increased, often markedly so in advanced disease.

Other typical spirometry findings in COPD include reduced FEV1, reduced peak expiratory flow rate, and less than 12% reversibility with bronchodilators. The severity of COPD is primarily classified based on the FEV1 as a percentage of predicted value.
learningPoints: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is characterised by obstructive spirometry patterns with a reduced FEV1/FVC ratio.
Air trapping in COPD leads to increased residual volume and total lung capacity.
Spirometry changes in COPD are not fully reversible with bronchodilators.
references: Johns DP, Walters JA, Walters EH. Diagnosis and early detection of COPD using spirometry. J Thorac Dis. 2014 Nov;6(11):1557-69. doi: 10.3978/j.issn.2072-1439.2014.08.18. PMID: 25478197; PMCID: PMC4255165.

Bailey KL. The importance of the assessment of pulmonary function in COPD. Med Clin North Am. 2012 Jul;96(4):745-52. doi: 10.1016/j.mcna.2012.04.011. Epub 2012 May 31. PMID: 22793942; PMCID: PMC3998207.
Macintyre NR. Spirometry for the diagnosis and management of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Respir Care. 2009 Aug;54(8):1050-7. Erratum in: Respir Care. 2009 Nov;54(11):1501. PMID: 19650946.

Athlin Å, Lisspers K, Hasselgren M, Ställberg B, Janson C, Montgomery S, Giezeman M, Kisiel M, Nager A, Sandelowsky H, Arne M, Sundh J. Diagnostic spirometry in COPD is increasing, a comparison of two Swedish cohorts. NPJ Prim Care Respir Med. 2023 Jun 2;33(1):23. doi: 10.1038/s41533-023-00345-8. PMID: 37264017; PMCID: PMC10235108.
